metadata.title: Morality without Consciousness
metadata.author: IanWS
metadata.epistemic-status: Considered but speculative. The intrinsic/extrinsic distinction in section 2 is the load-bearing move; I would defend it but expect reasonable disagreement.

term.gloss: An expressed interest counter to the second law of thermodynamics.

section.heading: Introduction
section.p: A recent post by Linch, "The Fourth World", gets at an important implication of consciousness — namely, that we ought to suspect further aspects of reality than we can today observe — but I am not sure it arrives there the right way.

The author makes two assumptions which ought not to come for free. Firstly, the author assumes that we could not explain consciousness through physicalism. This is already a strong claim, one with which about half of surveyed philosophers disagree.

section.heading: Phenomena are Intrinsic
section.p: If we suppose an intrinsic, physicalist theory of consciousness, then our experience of pain is by definition inseparable from the pain reaction itself.
section.inference: An intrinsic theory requires less deviation from contemporary neuroscience and sidesteps the problems of epiphenomenalism.
